coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for the determination and the differentiation of the rare earth element ions
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shengnan
Liu, Shuai
Zhang, Jianyu
Cao, Yan
coredata.dc:description: As indispensable and strategic elements, Rare Earth Elements (REEs) have been found wide high-tech applications in many modern industries. Their mining, extraction and purification address on economic and environmental significances. This study was the first to report the successful determination and differentiation of rare earth metals using the Nitrogen-doped Carbon Dots (NCDs). NCDs were synthesized via the one-step hydrothermal treatment method starting from folic acid. The as-received NCDs can emit bright blue fluorescence at 440 nm in a high quantum yield of 85% due to the fluorescence enhancement effect of N dopant atoms on NCDs’ surface. This as-received NCDs can not only determine REE ions based on the fluorescence quenching effect of the newly-formed REE-NCDs complexes, also distinguish two groups of REEs with significantly different electronic structures. It was found REE ions (Group II REEs: Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+) can only quench fluorescence of the initial NCDs solution, but not change its fluorescence wavelengths. This was contrasted to the similar quenching effect of REE ions (Group I REEs: La3+, Y3+, Lu3+) on NCDs, but further 20 nm red-shift of the fluorescence wavelength (from 439 nm to 458 nm). Taking examples of the selected La3+ and Sm3+, their detection limit were found to be 0.045 μM (the linear range of 0–6 μM, R2 =0.998) and 0.29 μM (the linear range of 0–10 μM, R2 =0.993), respectively. The characterizations of NCDs, based on the ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometry (UV–VIS), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), helped in revealing the coordination status of rare earth ions on the carboxyl group of the NCDs surface and also mechanisms of their electron and energy transfers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Rare earth elements
Fluorescence
Red-shift
Lanthanum
Samarium

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The determination and differentiation of REEs using NCDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key R&D Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Clinical Specialty Discipline Construction Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21676001 ), and the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2016YFE0108400 and 2017YFE0105500 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the as-received NCDs. Scale bar: 100 nm. (b) Size distribution diagram of the as-received NCDs. (c) XRD pattern of the as-received NCDs.
FTIR spectra of the as-received NCDs.
(a) Full XPS spectra of the as-received NCDs, (b) C 1 s XPS spectra, (c) N 1 s XPS spectra (d) O 1 s XPS spectra of the obtained NCDs product.

(a) Fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra of the as-received NCDs. Inset: photograph of the NCDs solution under irradiation with natural light (left) and UV light (right). (b) Fluorescence spectra of NCDs under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Fluorescence intensity change of the as-received NCDs in the presence of REE ions, various REE ions at a concentration of 100 μM (b) Fluorescence intensity ratio of the as-received NCDs in the presence of REE ions, various ions at a concentration of 100 μM (c) The change of Fluorescence emission spectra intensity and range of emission wavelength of the as-received NCDs in the presence of La3+(0–18 μM) (d) The change of Fluorescence emission spectra intensity of the as-received NCDs in the presence of Sm3+ (0–30 μM) (e) The change of fluorescence emission wavelength of the as-received NCDs in the presence of La3+(0–18 μM) (f) The linear relationship between fluorescence quenching of NCDs and the Sm3+ concentration.

(a) FT-IR spectrum of NCDs (black line) and their coordination product with La3+ (red line) their coordination product with Sm3+ (blue line). (b) High resolution C 1s XPS spectra of the NCDs, NCDs after reaction with La3+ and NCDs connected with Sm3+. (c) High resolution O 1s XPS spectra of the NCDs, NCDs after reaction with La3+ and NCDs connected with Sm3+. (d) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the as-received NCDs (black line), UV–Vis absorption spectra of the NCDs connected with La3+ (blue line), and UV–Vis absorption spectra of the NCDs connected with Sm3+ (red line).

Photoluminescence decay curve of NCDs at emission of 440 nm (black line), Photoluminescence decay curve of NCDs-Sm3+ at emission of 440 nm (red line) and Photoluminescence decay curve of NCDs-La3+ at emission of 458 nm (blue line).

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for the determination and the differentiation of the rare earth element ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Combustion Science and Environmental Technology, Department of Chemistry, Western Kentucky University, Bowling Green, KY 42101, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Combustion Science and Environmental Technology, Department of Chemistry, Western Kentucky University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute for Combustion Science and Environmental Technology, Department of Chemistry, Western Kentucky University, Bowling Green, KY 42101, USA .

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Combustion Science and Environmental Technology, Department of Chemistry, Western Kentucky University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As indispensable and strategic elements, Rare Earth Elements (REEs) have been found wide high-tech applications in many modern industries. Their mining, extraction and purification address on economic and environmental significances. This study was the first to report the successful determination and differentiation of rare earth metals using the Nitrogen-doped Carbon Dots (NCDs). NCDs were synthesized via the one-step hydrothermal treatment method starting from folic acid. The as-received NCDs can emit bright blue fluorescence at 440 nm in a high quantum yield of 85% due to the fluorescence enhancement effect of N dopant atoms on NCDs’ surface. This as-received NCDs can not only determine REE ions based on the fluorescence quenching effect of the newly-formed REE-NCDs complexes, also distinguish two groups of REEs with significantly different electronic structures. It was found REE ions (Group II REEs: Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+) can only quench fluorescence of the initial NCDs solution, but not change its fluorescence wavelengths. This was contrasted to the similar quenching effect of REE ions (Group I REEs: La3+, Y3+, Lu3+) on NCDs, but further 20 nm red-shift of the fluorescence wavelength (from 439 nm to 458 nm). Taking examples of the selected La3+ and Sm3+, their detection limit were found to be 0.045 μM (the linear range of 0–6 μM, R2 =0.998) and 0.29 μM (the linear range of 0–10 μM, R2 =0.993), respectively. The characterizations of NCDs, based on the ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometry (UV–VIS), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), helped in revealing the coordination status of rare earth ions on the carboxyl group of the NCDs surface and also mechanisms of their electron and energy transfers.

ce:section.ce:para: Rare Earth Elements (REEs), are indispensable elements in many modern industries, has become a crucial strategic resource for the 21st century. Rare earth elements have wide high-tech applications in optics, electricity, magnetism, nuclear radiation and other fields due to unique physical and chemical properties [1–4]. Their mining, extraction and purification address on economic and environmental significances.

Rare earth elements (REEs) refer to 15 lanthanides (atomic numbers 57–71) on the periodic table, and scandium (Sc) and yttrium (Y) are often regarded as rare earth elements because of their similarity in nature. Traditionally, according to the properties of rare earth elements, it can be divided into two groups: Light rare earth (LREE) (lanthanum (La), cerium (Ce), praseodymium (Pr), neodymium (Nd), promethium (Pm), samarium (Sm) and europium (Eu)) and heavy rare earth (HREE) (gadolinium (Gd), terbium (Tb), dysprosium (Dy), holmium (Ho), erbium (Er), thulium (Tm), ytterbium (Yb), lutetium (Lu), yttrium (Y)). In compounds, lanthanides can exhibit different valences II, III and IV. The positive trivalent state (III) is the most typical and stable valence state [5], in which two 6 S electrons and 5d electrons can be lost to become a trivalent rare earth ion Ln3+, and their electronic layout state is [XE] 4fn. According to the electronic layout state of rare earth trivalent ions, trivalent REE ions can be classified into two specific categories: Group I (Y3+, La3+, Lu3+) and the Group II (Ce3+,Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+). More specifically, there is no 4f layer electron in the electronic configuration of Group I, whose electronic layout state is 4f0, trivalent lutetium (Lu3+) is [XE] 4f14, but Group II REE ions have unpaired electrons.

The current detection methods of REE elements cover gravimetric analysis [6], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [7], ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry(UV–vis) [8], fluorescence spectroscopy [9], X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (XRF)[10], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), etc. [11]. Because of great similarity of trivalent REE ions’ electronic layout state (4fn), their differentiation is generally difficult. Leading their separation generally a challenging task. In terms of the analytical techniques for individual separation of lanthanides, the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is generally applied based on bonded phase cation exchangers [12,13], which is technically challenging and time-consuming. This calls for more simple and rapid methods for the precisely distinguishing the rare earth and determining their concentrations.

The fluorescent method likely provides attractive solution toward REEs’ determination in the high sensitivity, selectivity, and the wide linear range. “Carbon”, one of the most abundant elements in the universe, is easily accessible. Its allotropes include covers 3-dimensional (3-D) graphite, diamond and amorphous carbon. Their new members’ covers 0-D fullerenes, 1-D carbon nanotubes and 2-D graphene which have attracted much attention due to their potential applications. Another new member in the carbon family is the 0-D carbon dots which are grabbing more interests. The carbon dots (CDs) are carbon nanoparticles with almost spherical geometry, and generally possess a sp2 conjugated core and contain suitable oxygen content that consisting of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and aldehyde groups. Carbon Dots include carbon quantum dots and graphene quantum dots. Their excellent luminescence properties make them to be a new class of fluorescence agents. Compared to other available fluorescence nanomaterial such as inorganic semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), gold/silver nano-clusters, CDs hold many advantages. Carbon dots are relatively easy to synthesize, and have stable emissions not suffering from photo-bleaching. Thus, publications on carbon-dots have experience an explosive increase since 2006, primarily due to merits of their unique sizes, facile preparation and capable surface modification, better water-solubility, less toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, capable being both excellent electron donors and acceptors [14–16]. These excellence features make CDs especially useful for fluorescent bio-sensing or imaging, optical detection probes [17–20], nano-medicine [21,22], light emitting diode materials and photo-catalysts [23].

The methodology for fabrication of CDs can be divided into two main categories: physical synthesis and chemical synthesis. The former involves the arc discharge method [24] and the laser ablation technique [25,26], and the latter includes the oxidation method [27], the hydrothermal treatment [28–30], the microwave method [31,32], the Electro- chemical synthesis [33–35], the carbonization technique [36,37] and etc. The hydrothermal synthesis is widely accepted due to its advantages in mild and simple reaction conditions.

This work presented a facile, low-cost and one-step hydrothermal carbonization route for the preparation of water-soluble luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) only using folic acid (FA) reagent, which is used as both carbon and nitrogen source. The resulting negatively charged NCDs can emit the unique bright blue fluorescence in a high fluorescence quantum yield. They were subsequently applied to identify the concentrations of REE ions and distinguish different REE ions. To our best knowledge, there were almost no literatures reporting on distinguishing and detecting rare earth ions using CDs.

Folic acid (FA, C19H19N7O6), quinine sulfate and sulfuric acid were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water supplied from a Nanopure water purification water system (18.25 MΩ cm). The REEs salts used for testing were also purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation, and used as received. Standard stock solutions of REE ions ( Sc3+, Y3+, La3+, Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, Lu3+) were prepared using ultrapure water from the respective REE nitrate salts (Sc(NO3)3, Y(NO3)3, La(NO3)3, Pr(NO3)3, Nd(NO3)3, Sm(NO3)3, Eu(NO3)3, Gd(NO3)3, Tb(NO3)3, Dy(NO3)3, Ho(NO3)3, Er(NO3)3, Tm(NO3)3, Yb(NO3)3, Lu(NO3)3). All chemical reagents were used without further purification.

The negatively charged NCDs were synthesized via a one-step simple hydrothermal refer to literature [28,32]. Nitrogen-doped CDs were synthesized directly from Folic acid (FA) using a one-step simple hydrothermal treatment. Typically, the negatively charged NCDs were prepared as follows: Firstly, 0.1 g FA were dissolved into 100 mL distilled water under vigorously stirring to form a homogeneous solution, then the mixture homogeneous solution was transferred into Teflon lined stainless autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 180 °C in an electric heater and kept for additional 6 h while the solution changed from opaque to transparent and yellow-brown, indicating the carbonization of the reactant. This process indicated the formation of NCDs. After cooling to the room temperature, the as-received NCDs solution was filtered via a 0.45 µm membrane to remove large or agglomerated particles. The obtained clear brown solution was utilized for further purification. The C-dots solution was further filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove constituents larger than 220 nm. The further purification was subjected to the dialysis process against purification water through a membrane (MWCO = 500-1000Da) for 10 h. Finally, the clear, light, yellow–brown NCDs aqueous was concentrated using a rotary evaporation (RE-52AA) and dried by Freeze Drying Machine (Lab-1–80) to obtain yellow solid NCDs.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were investigated for all tested samples using a JEM-2100 microscope to investigate the microstructures and size distribution. The chemical bonds and components of the NCDs were investigated using an IR Prestige-21 (FT-IR) in the frequency range of 4000–500 cm−1 and an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted on a Smart Lab 9 kW diffract meter with Cu Kα radiation and a scanning rate of 10°/min (40 kV, 40 mA). The Zeta potential of the NCDs have investigated using A ZETASZER ZS90. The Ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometry (UV–VIS) and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV 3600 spectrophotometer and an F-2500 (Hitachi, Japan) fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. The absolute quantum yield and relative quantum yield were also recorded on a full-function steady state transient fluorescence spectrometer (F-2500 Hitachi, Japan) using quinine sulfate as a reference, respectively.

The quantum yield (Qsam) of the NCDs was determined by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensities (excited at 280 nm) and the absorbance values (at 280 nm) of the NCDs using quinine sulfate as a reference. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was calculated according to the following equation:
Q
sam
=
Q
ref
×
I
sam
I
ref
×
A
ref
A
sam

Where subscripts “ref” and “sam” denote standard (quinine sulfate) and tested samples (NCDs) respectively, Q is fluorescence quantum yield. Where Q ref and Q sam are the quantum yield of the standard substance and the NCDs. In brief, a solution of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Q ref = 54%) was used as a standard. A ref and A sam respectively stand for the integral fluorescence intensity of the diluted standard solution and the tested NCDs solution; I sam and I ref are the absorbance value of NCDs solution and the diluted standard solution, respectively. In order to minimize interference, both individual absorbance of the quinine sulfate standard and as-received NCDs were adjusted down to 0.05 at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The determination and differentiation of REEs using NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Referred to the blank NCDs solution, changes of the fluorescence intensity and wavelength could be measured upon addition of REE ions into the NCDs solutions. This study addressed the presence of REE ions (Y3+, La3+, Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, Lu3+) in the as-received NCDs. The specific attention were given to two typical REEs (Sm3+and La3+) to identify potentials of the as-received NCDs to distinguish REE ions based on their electronic layout state of REE trivalent ions. The applied concentrations of La3+ ions ranged from 0 to 18 μM, and Sm3+ from 0 to 30 μM. The linear regression and detection limits can be obtained via corresponding to the intensity of the NCDs solutions and REEs’ concentrations.

A series of characterizations were performed to study the elements, structure, morphology and surface groups of the as-received NCDs. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) results helped to optimize synthesis conditions toward controls of sizes and morphology of all the NCDs samples. Transmission electron microscope images are shown in Fig. 1a, revealing the torispherical-like morphology and the uniform dispersion of the NCDs in water (no sediment in solution) with a size in the range of 11–23 nm. The size distribution is depicted in Fig. 1b by counting NCDs in TEM images, showing the mean diameter of NCDs was approximately 15 nm. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of NCDs in Fig. 1c displayed a broad peak (referring to the carbon phase 002) at 27°, corresponding to a graphite structure [32,38–40]. This peak can be calculated the spacing distance of the NCDs which was 0.33 nm based on the Bragg's Law (2dsinθ = n λ).

To determine surface states and compositions of the as-received NCDs, characterizations using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Zeta potential were performed. Fig. 2a shows the FT-IR spectra of NCDs. The result indicated a broad absorption band at around 3400 cm−1, referring to the amino (-NH2) and hydroxyl (-OH) functional groups on the surface of NCDs. Peaks at 2920 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 referred to stretching vibration C-H bond, and peaks at 1660, 1600, and 1085 cm−1 can be attributed to stretching vibrations of bonds C˭N/C˭O, C-C, and C-O-C, respectively [41]. The bimodal stretching vibration peak of –NH appeared at 1384 cm−1. Fig. S1 (Supporting information) shows the obvious difference of FT-IR spectra between raw materials FA and the as-received NCDs, meaning the completeness of the conversion from raw material (FA) toward NCDs.

The surface groups’ characterization by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) determined the valence states of elements in carbon materials and contents of elements, as shown in Fig. 3. Results revealed that the relative atomic percentages of C, N, and O in the as-received NCDs were 52.2%, 27.2%, and 20.7%, respectively. Fig. 3a shows three typical sharp peaks of graphitic C 1s (285 eV), N 1 s (400 eV) and O 1 s (532 eV), illustrating the presence of rich groups of O and N on the surface of the carbon frame. The abundant oxygen-containing groups ensured the good water solubility of the as-received NCDs, which was favorable for the further combination with the target REE ions in aqueous environment. The peaks at 287.99 eV, 286.32 eV, 285.48 eV and 284.70 eV in high-resolution C1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3b) corresponded to C˭O (-COOH), C-OH, C-N, and C−C/C˭C (sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon), respectively [28]. This was well consistent to FT-IR results. Fig. 3c shows the high resolution spectra of N 1 s. Three peaks with binding energies of 402.19 eV, 401.45 eV, and 399.65 eV can be assigned to amino N, graphitic-N and pyridine-like, respectively [42,43]. Two fitting peaks referring to O 1 s at 531.33 and 532.68 eV were attributed to C˭O and C−OH/C−O−C bonds, as shown in Fig. 3d [44].

The Zeta potential for NCDs was measured to study the nature of their surfaces as well as their electron density, as shown in Fig. 4. Zeta potential values for the tested NCDs were − 14.4 mV, revealing that it's negatively charged surfaces, due to doping of two electron rich groups on the surface (N-rich groups on the surface and abundant oxygen-containing groups). The high negative charge on their surface, together with highly-water solubility endowed the as-received NCDs with the good affinity to bonding of positively-charged REEs in water.

The optical properties of the as-received NCDs were investigated using ultraviolet-visible (UV–VIS, blue line) spectroscopy, fluorescence emission spectroscopy (red line), and fluorescence excitation measurements (green line), respectively, as shown in Fig. 5. Fig. 5(a), UV–VIS absorption spectrum of the NCDs in water, presented three typical absorption peaks at 235 nm, 280 nm, 335 nm, and a small band at 205 nm. The most typical characteristic absorption of NCDs was around 280 nm, likely attributed to the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states, and the typical absorption of an aromatic π system of the carbonyl (C˭O) [45]. The other two absorption peaks at 235 and 335 nm can be assigned to π-π * transition of aromatic sp2 domains including C˭C or C˭N bond and n-π* transition of C˭O bond, respectively [46]. The small absorption peak at 205 nm may be attributed to n-δ* transition, indicating the presence of NH2 [41]. From the photographs shown in Fig. 5a inset, the diluted NCDs aqueous solution was pale yellow under ambient light (inset, Fig. 5(a) left) and fluorescence spectra manifested that NCDs aqueous solution can emit strong blue fluorescence under the UV light (inset, Fig. 5(a) right). As Fig. 5a shown, an optical absorption spectra (blue line) peak at 280 nm matches with the maximum fluorescence excitation (green line). In aqueous solution, the NCDs exhibited blue fluorescence with maximum emission at 440 nm (red line).

It's common for fluorescent carbon materials to have excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviors. However, no perceptible shift of the emission wavelength in the fluorescence intensity was observed in the excitation wavelength varied from 275 nm to 400 nm (Fig. 5b), illustrating an excitation-independent emission of the as-received NCDs, which was completely different from most regular CDs [28,29]. The maximum emission of the as-received NCDs was found at 440 nm with an excitation wavelength of 280 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of the as received NCDs was found to be about 85% using quinine sulfate as a reference, indicating the high quantum yield of the as-received NCDs. The nitrogen element in the raw material is mixed with the carbon skeleton and surface of the carbon dot, which may further improve high fluorescence and high quantum yield [47]. The fluorescence lifetime also showed excitation independent and remained constant under excitation of 280–400 nm. The fluorescence decay of the as-received NCDs was calculated using a monoexponential decay function to yield a lifetime of 4.86 ns, with emission wavelength of 440 nm (in Fig. S2 of the Supporting information).

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used for the detection of various metal ions based on mechanisms of fluorescence quenching induced by the combination of metal ion in CDs. However, so far there were almost no literatures reporting on distinguishing and detecting REEs using CDs. Fig. 6a shows the changes of the fluorescence intensity of the as-received NCDs in the presence of REE ions (Y3+, La3+, Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, Lu3+), to identify potentials of the as-received NCDs to distinguish REE ions. Referred to the blank NCDs solution, the change of the fluorescence intensity could be obviously observed upon addition of REE ions into the NCDs solutions (Fig. 6b). Most importantly, the bonding of NCDs with the different REE ions obviously exhibited different fluorescence phenomenon, revealing a promise to a step-forward distinguish the very similar REE ions. According to the electronic layout state of REE trivalent ions, trivalent REE ions can be classified into two special categories: Group I (Y3+, La3+ and Lu3+) and Group II (Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+and Yb3+). Our results exhibited the Group I REE ions (La3+, Y3+, and Lu3+) lead a clear shift of their wavelengths of fluorescence emission peak in NCDs solutions from 440 nm to 458 nm. This was different from the quenched fluorescence intensity by about 85% of that of initial NCDs solution when Group II REE ions (Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+) in 100 μM were applied. From the Fig. 6a, it can manifest that the REE ions in the same group had similar quenching effects on the fluorescence of carbon dots. Therefore, further study only selected and investigated La3+ (representing Group I REE ions) and Sm3+ (representing Group II REE ions). The significant difference within these two REEs groups can be also seen in photographs (Fig. 6b inset), displaying that La3+ ions into NCDs solution can emit strong yellow-green fluorescence (inset, Fig. 6(b) middle), versus that Sm3+ ions at the same concentration into NCDs solution can be more effectively quenched and thus only very weak fluorescence was received (inset, Fig. 6(b) right). Fig. 6c quantitatively displays the fluorescent emission spectra of NCDs upon addition of La3+ ions different concentrations from 0 to 18 μM. As expected, the addition of La3+ ions into the as-received NCDs lead a red-shift of fluorescence of NCDs from 439 nm to 458 nm. Fig. 6d manifests that the usual quenching effect of Sm3+ions on the fluorescence of the as-received NCDs, meaning that the peak intensity of the emission decreased as the concentration of the Sm3+ (0–30 μM) ions were increased, but there was no shift of emission peak wavelength. In particular, Fig. 6e displays that Fluorescence emission wavelength of the as-received NCDs in the presence of La3+ (0–18 μM) changes from 439 nm to 458 nm. Fig. 6e inset displays that a good linear relationship (linear correlation, R2 =0.9980) exists between the fluorescence emission wavelength of NCDs and the La3+ under the concentration range from 0 to 6 μM. After taking the log aggression, the linear regression can be obtained as: W-Wmin/ Wmax-Wmin= 0.4245logCLa + 3.1188, the calculated detection limits of La3+ was 0.045 μM. Wmin is the emission wavelength of the blank NCDs solutions, Wmax is the emission wavelength of the NCDs solutions with excessive La3+ ions, W is the emission wavelength of NCDs with La3+ under the concentration range from 0 to 6 μM. Fig. 6f displays that a good linear relationship (linear correlation, R2 =0.9930) exists between the quenching degree of NCDs and the Sm3+under the concentration range from 0 to 10 μM. The linear regression can be obtained as: F0/F=0.10729CSm + 1.0825. F0 is the intensity of the NCDs solutions, F is the intensity of NCDs with Sm3+ with different concentrations. The detection limited for Sm3+ is calculation from fluorescence quenching experiment of NCDs with concentration of Sm3+. From Fig. 6f inset, the calculated detection limits of Sm3+ was 0.29 μM.

Numerous studies explored mechanisms on the fluorescence phenomenon of carbon dots, finding either fluorescence quenching or enhancing phenomenon of carbon dots [17,48]. In this study, all REEs can interact with NCDs effectively, but two REE groups showed significantly different pheromones. As previously shown in Fig. 6a, the Sm3+ ions efficiently quenched the NCDs fluorescence emission at 440 nm, and similar to La3+ at the same conditions, but the fluorescence emission peak wavelength red-shifted by about 20 nm when both were added into the NCDs solution. The further studies based on FTIR, XPS, UV–VIS absorption spectra and zeta potential confirmed the different interactions between La-NCDs and Sm-NCDs, revealing their different interaction mechanisms.

The FTIR spectra showed the as-received NCDs owning plenty of amine, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surface. Amine, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups can easily coordinate with REE ions due to rich electrons are on the surface. The zeta potential of the as received NCDs was −14.4 mV, showing their negatively surface charged and thus likely electrostatic-interacting and electron transfer occurred between negatively NCDs and the positively charged REE ions. Therefore, it's likely that multiple amino, carbonyls or hydroxyls coordinate with REE ions such as La3+ or Sm3+. In principle, REE ions displayed a certain affinity to the oxygen-donor atoms and thus can coordinate with the carboxyl groups [49]. FTIR and XPS further confirmed the carboxyl on the surface of the as-received NCDs mainly coordinated with REE ions. Fig. 7a shows the changes in FTIR spectra of NCDs before and after the applied La3+ (blue line) or Sm3+ (red line) in NCDs solutions. Comparing to the NCDs base line, a moderately strong and wide peak was weakened to be weak peak at around 1660 cm−1 and a moderately strong and wide peak ranged at 1380 cm−1 to 1460 cm−1 was weakened to a sharp peak at 1380 cm−1. Refer to the analysis FT-IR spectrum of Fig. 2, further revealing that selective coordination of Sm3+ or La3+ with surface carbonyls (C˭O)groups on NCDs

Fig. 7b and Fig. 7c shows the changes in high-resolution C and O spectra of NCDs before and after the addition of La3+ or Sm3+. The XPS peak shifting of NCD-La3+ was very similar to that of NCD-Sm3+ with an identifiable differences. Comparing to the NCDs base line, the peak of C1s at 286.32ev disappeared completely and the new peak at 289.72 eV significantly increased (In the Fig. 7b, peak of Sm3+-NCDs). This was an indication of the stronger Sm3+ interacting on oxygen atom of carboxyl group in NCDs and the enhanced energy transfer from the NCDs to Sm3+, leading the fluorescence quenching. Similarly in Fig. 7b (the peak of La3+-NCDs), the peak of C1s at 286.32 greatly decreased and the new peak at a slightly different 289.62 eV obviously increased. The formed REE-NCD complexes can facilitate electron transfer and restrain the excited recombination of excitations, leading to remarkable quenching effects on fluorescence intensity of NCDs and emission peak wavelength red-shift in some case. Further Fig. 7c (the peak of O1s) also confirmed Sm3+ or La3+ interacted on oxygen atom of carboxyl group in NCDs, due to the decreased peak area at 531ev. The details of each XPS peaks of compounds are shown in Supporting Table S1 and Table S2.

Furthermore, absorption peaks of the as-received NCDs alone and the applied La3+-NCD and in the applied Sm3+ -NCD are illustrated in Fig. 7d. It indicated the addition of La3+ or the addition of Sm3+ lead the increases of absorbance intensities of the NCDs solutions at 235 nm and 280 nm and the blue-shift of these two peaks. This observation revealed that the ground-state complexes was likely generated due to the interaction between La3+ and Sm3+ with the NCDs [50]. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime decay spectrum is shown in Fig. 8, the fluorescence lifetime of NCDs was found 4.9 ns (the black line). Nevertheless, the fluorescence lifetime of the NCDs solution with Sm3+ was found shorter in 2.8 ns (the red lines), unlike trivalent Sm3+, the fluorescence lifetime of the NCDs solution added into the same concentration La3+ was found longer in 5.4 ns (the blue line). This clearly revealed the different fluorescence mechanisms of interaction of two REE ions with NCDs. The possible fluorescence mechanisms can be interpreted in the term of the surface structural characteristic of the NCDs. It's generally acceptable that the molecular structure can impact on the fluorescence quantum yield, involving electron or energy transfer, collisional quenching, excited-state reactions, and ground-state complex formations [51].

The as-received NCDs with rich electron easily interacted with REE ions, leading the formation of a new stable complex, within which the rich electrons on the surface of NCDs can delocalize to REE ions by electron or energy transfer and thereby increase the electronegativity of the REE ions. The newly formed complexes, combining REEs and NCDs, had new chemical structures and properties because of unique electronic layout state of individual REE ions. There was first reported the covalent bonding characteristics of REE compounds in 1975 [52]. Therefore, the combination of REE ions and NCDs likely have either ionic or covalent bonding structures. The electronic layout state of trivalent lanthanide ions is [XE] 4fn. Some REE ions, shows “metallic” in combining with NCDs, forming dominant ionic bonds and weak covalent bonds, the other REE ions, shows "nonmetallic" in combining with NCDs, forming more covalent bonds. For example, Sm3+ in the electronic layout state of 4f5 is a “metallic” REE ion contrasting to La3+ in 4f°, which is more “nonmetallic” REE ion. The former one is unstable and the electron-deficiency, and the later one was more stable with the electron filled state. The metallic property of the Sm3+ can accept electrons from the NCDs to its unoccupied orbital leading a stable state. This newly formed Sm3+-NCDs complex can’t shift emission peak wavelength of NCDs, because NCDs with Sm3+ did not change the bonding structure of NCDs, but only changes the electronic quantity of NCDs. This was not true for the La3+-NCDs complex. La3+ ion did not easily accept electrons, but shared a certain amount of electrons to the negatively charged NCDs due to the electronegativity effect of La3+, leading the formation of a large π-conjugated La3+-NCDs complex. The formed La3+-NCDs conjugated bond changed the chemical structure of NCDs, occurred a red shift in the fluorescence spectrum of NCDs. Our previous studies based on XPS and fluorescence lifetime confirmed the different fluorescence mechanisms of interaction of these two types of REE ions with NCDs.

As Table S1 presented, the peak of C1s near 286.32 eV in XPS spectrum of Sm3+-NCDs disappeared completely and that of La3+-NCDs was also greatly decreased, compared to that of significant C1s peak at 286.32 eV of NCDs. The Fig. 3b ascribed the peak of C1s at 286.32 eV to C˭O (-COOH). Therefore, Sm3+ in the Sm3+-NCDs complex was stronger to obtain electrons of carboxyl than La3+ in La3+-NCDs. There was no shift when Sm3+ combined with NCDs but a red shift of emission peak wavelength when the La3+ combined with NCDs. Therefore, we predicted the existence of the original 286.3 eV of NCDs in Sm3+-NCDs, but slightly red-shift of La3+-NCDs to 286.2 eV in their XPS spectrum. This was successfully confirmed by XPS spectra, as shown in Table S1. REEs interacted carboxyl (C˭O) decreased electrons density on the surface of NCDs and Increased the corresponding binding energy. Detailed studies on XPS spectra of Sm3+-NCDs revealed newly appearing peak at 289.72 eV corresponding to C1s in C˭O-Sm, versus to that the newly appearing peak at 289.62 eV corresponding to that in C˭O-La. This new bonding energy of C˭O-La at 289.6 eV was about 3.4 eV greater than the bonding energy of C˭O. The bonding energy in Sm3+-NCDs was 0.10 eV greater than La3+-NCDs, revealing that Sm3+ has a stronger ability to get electron from carboxyl of NCDs than La3+. Thus, we hypothesized that there were likely changes of adsorptions or emissions in the near infrared area for both Sm3+-NCDs and La3+-NCDs, and also the red shift about 20 nm of the La3+-NCDs from that of Sm3+-NCDs.

Secondly, the fluorescence lifetime of the original NCDs was 4.9 ns, that of the Sm3+-NCDs was shortened to be 2.8 ns, and that of the La3+-NCDs was increased to be 5.4 ns. The conjugation of Sm3+ ions on NCDs decreased numbers of either excited electrons on the surface of NCDs, or bypass excited electrons on NCDs toward Sm3+. Thus, the less excited electrons clearly decreased the lifetime of fluorescence based on the theory of the quenching mechanism [53]. In contrast, a greater conjugated bond was formed between La3+ and NCDs. leading a more stable chemical structure and further enhancement of the binding force between electrons in excited states on NCDs. The conjugated system had the higher electron delocalization and lower transition energy, thus the more electrons can transition to excited state, and enhanced fluorescence lifetime of the complex La3+-NCDs to 5.4 ns and the emission peak was also red-shifted [54]. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime can proved further the electronic layout state of 4f1–13, was a “metallic” REE ion contrasting to others more “nonmetallic” REE ions in the electronic layout state of 4f0 or 4f14.

In summary, the following conclusion can be drawn,
(1)
Nitrogen-doped Carbon dots (NCDs) were successfully synthesized by the single-step hydrothermal method using a single-source folic acid;
(2)
The as-received NCDs, with their rich electron, were negatively charged and water soluble;
(3)
The NCDs emitted a bright blue fluorescence in a quantum yield of 85% under the UV radiation;
(4)
The as-received NCDs can help distinguish Group I and II REEs. NCDs can effectively interact with REEs in different mechanisms. The conjugation of NCDs with Group I REE ions (La3+, Y3+, and Lu3+), typically La3+-NCDs, performed not only quenching but also a clear red-shift of the fluorescence wavelength from 439 nm to 458 nm, that can be ascribed to a “nonmetallic” behavior.
(5)
As for the Group II REE ions (Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+ and Yb3+), their conjugation with NCDs, typically Sm3+-NCDs, performed no red-shift, but only fluorescence quenching effect, that can be ascribed to a more “metallic” behavior.
(6)
Studies via FTIR, XPS, zeta potential and fluorescence lifetimes of the conjugated REEs-NCDs further provided insights on their different mechanisms.
(7)
The detection limit of La3+ was 0.045 μM in a linear range of 0–6 μM (the linear correlation, R2 =0.9980), and the detection limit of Sm3+ was 0.29 μM in a linear range of 0–10 μM (the linear correlation, R2 =0.9930).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21676001), and the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2016YFE0108400 and 2017YFE0105500).

ce:section.ce:para: All authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2019.01.113.
